text: The presence of 7α-hydroxypregnenolone in the brain is associated with locomotor activity in the salmon and in the male bird and newt. 7α-hydroxypregnenolone is a neurosteroid synthesized from pregnenolone by CYP7B in vertebrates including bird, newt, and fish. When 7α-hydroxypregnenolone concentration increases in the brain (endogenous or exogenous), these animals become active. Oppositely, decreased synthesis of 7α-hydroxypregnenolone limits locomotor activity (Matsunaga et al., 2004).    The importance of 7α-hydroxypregnenolone synthesis is sex dependent in bird and newt. In these species, only male locomotor activity is influenced by the neurosteroid (Matsunaga et al., 2004, Tsutsui et al., 2008). However, both male and female are affected by 7α-hydroxypregnenolone in salmon (haraguchi et al., 2015).   It was known before that locomotor activity in vertebrates fluctuated over a circadian and/or seasonal cycle, although the full mechanism was elusive (Saper et al., 2005). The discovery of 7α-hydroxypregnenolone activity in the brain allowed a better understanding of the locomotor activity regulation in the context of cyclic variations of the environment.
